g.data-narration: Welcome to Formatted Input/Output in C++. This topic covers stream classes, manipulators, and formatting flags.
Formatted I/O controls how data appears when displayed or read. It enables precise control over numbers, strings, and output presentation.
g.text: Formatted I/O controls how data is displayed and read in C++ programs.
The iostream library provides: cin (input), cout (output),
cerr and clog for error handling.

g.data-narration: Formatting makes output readable with tables, alignment, and proper number bases. It helps create user-friendly applications and professional reports.
g.text: • Create user-friendly applications with readable output
• Generate professional reports and tabular data
• Debug programs by controlling number bases (hex, octal)

g.data-narration: The C++ I/O system uses a class hierarchy. Base class ios_base provides common functionality, while ios adds formatting state and error handling.
Format flags control I/O behavior. boolalpha shows booleans as true/false. hex, oct, dec set integer number bases.
Show flags display additional information: showbase shows 0x for hex, showpoint shows decimal point, showpos shows + for positive numbers.
Scientific notation uses exponential form, fixed uses decimal notation. Uppercase controls hex letters and scientific notation case.
Alignment flags control text positioning: left aligns padding on right, right aligns padding on left, internal pads between sign and number.
No-argument manipulators like endl, flush, and ws modify stream behavior. endl outputs newline and flushes the buffer.
setw(n) sets field width for the next output only. It's non-sticky unlike other manipulators. Works with strings too.
setprecision(n) controls floating-point display. In default mode it sets significant digits, in fixed mode it sets decimal places.
g.text: Remember: Default mode = significant digits | Fixed mode = decimal places

g.data-narration: setbase sets integer base (8, 10, 16). setiosflags sets multiple format flags at once using bitmask.
Member functions like width(), precision(), fill() provide another way to control formatting. They work alongside manipulators.
Programmers can create custom manipulators. Simple manipulators return stream reference. Parameterized ones use a helper class.
Always check stream state using fail(), eof(), good() before processing input. This handles invalid data gracefully.
Understanding sticky vs non-sticky is crucial. setw is non-sticky (next operation only), while precision, base, and alignment are sticky.
g.text: hex, oct, dec, scientific, fixed, setprecision, setfill, left, right, internal, boolalpha, showbase, showpoint, showpos

g.data-narration: Remember: Use setw before each output, switch back to dec after hex, and always include iomanip for parameterized manipulators.
g.text: • setw() is non-sticky - use before each output
• hex/oct/dec are sticky - remember to reset to dec
• setprecision: default = sig-digits, fixed = decimal places
Master these concepts for university exams!
